posts.title: Reading a Clock
posts.summary: a simple project in order to teach how to read a clock
posts.body:
links: 

view repo

Try it!

Summary:

My sister is learning how to read an analog clock, so figure I did throw this together real quick for her. I added three modes: 
  • Exploration, for when you just want to get an image of a desired time. 
  • Problem, so the kiddo can just type in and answer the prompted question
  • Test, something I made for myself, where 10 problems are given and timed. This is to satisfy my desire to be competitive.

posts.title: Go themed QR code maker
posts.summary: A simple that returns things to its origin
posts.body:
View repo

Try it yourself!

Lore time: 

Just about everyone has used a QR code before, in fact there is a chance you found this page through a QR code. QR, short for quick response, is a way information is laid out in such a way it can pass on information quickly through a camera. Because of this, they are very good to pass on an URL to another human, where the accuracy required and length of the links makes things difficult. As the fable goes, is that the inventor was a Go player and took inspiration from a Go board.  A go game is a series of information, and has the advantage of being readable in any angle (unlike a barcode). [American Go Association]

I myself am a Go player, but I didn't find out about this fact until Veritasium made a video on the topic. It was mildly hard to watch him placing the stones into the squares, when the game is played with stones placed on the intersections. However, it did inspire me to make this tool. [youtube]

The making/using: 

After trying to find tools that does this and failing, I decided to make the tool myself. I won't be surprised if there are existing services out there, but they just aren't search optimized. Not like mine version ended up being findable. However I do intent on sharing this tool with mostly people who aren't fellow software people, so there are some design challenges. 

I had some goals going in making this: 
  • Everyone should be able to use this, the design should be intuitive and simple. 
  • I don't want to make people download anything. I myself don't want to deal with it running in different environments, and they don't want to download some random thing a stranger made. 
  • It should look pleasing to the eye. 

So that lead to the design I ended up going with:

A simple two panel design, one where you toggle, this will act as the mental model for input. The other, showing a preview with minimal actions you can perform. I fully expect people to just screen shot the QR code instead of using the download button. So I kept it simple. 

Everything is statically hosted, so I can just send them a link. 

Some stylistic choices: 

The corners doesn't have to be so out of place like that, however, if its just a board full of stones it might not be straight forward to the viewer that this is a QR code. I have since then added an option to change that.  
The stones are simple black and white, which are convincingly go stones enough by themselves. There is a set of slate and shell set, which would represent a more fancy set of go stones available, but the images used here are on the dimmer and murky side. 

Stuff that I learned: 

Did you know you can just cover up a chunk of the QR code and it still works? The logo option here just overlay an image on top of the QR with no change to the QR itself, and it still works perfect with parts of it hidden. 

I think this was the first tool that I made and shared publicly with a large group of people. I posted it in Beginner Go Club discord server, and the San Diego Go Club discord, and I received some positive feedback. I think this was the catalyst for much of my other projects.

posts.title: How did we get to Yuda.work?
posts.summary: A little overview of how this website was made!
posts.body:
Why does this exist?

Everyone needs a website nowadays, at least that's what they tell me. So, around early 2026, I bought the domain Yuda.work as a bit of a birthday gift to myself. However, work on the website didn't start until when I discovered some stuff recently. In a way, the only reason this exist, is because I have the necessary materials (the domain) and I have the skill set to do it. 

If you want to look over the code yourself:
GitHub Link: 

Repo

Project Result Link: 

yuda.work

How was it made?

Aside from all the youtuber blasting me with website builder/hosting services, where words like "essential for a business" and "online presence" are dropped, I've only spend very short amount of time thinking about website making. Sure, there are moments where I did some soul searching about whether wanting things and thus needing money made me a 1 person business, but I've never really see the true point of it. 

I had some assumption about websites:
  • It cost money to host! so if it doesn't give that back to me why should I make one?
  • It needs to be hosted somehow? Is this really worth learning to host a server for?
  • When was the last time you actually stumbled onto someone's personal website?
These however, were not true. Turns out, one of the best part about programming is you can make exactly what you need. 

A good friend showed me his own website that he put on his resume, which is by far the leading inspiration for me making this one. The website is under Heston.sh, though his website is much more polished. He's got a "this character has these stats" theme going on, except its him and his skills. During it being shown to me, he mentioned that GitHub can just host pages for you, for free. 

Of course, with not knowing what you are missing out on, there is not a whole lot of seeking. I didn't look into static hosting on GitHub pages until I had to a project on it. 

A good friend approached me about making a "multiplayer clock". Long story short, it was modifying an existing project, and it exists as a GitHub pages. There was nothing but one HTML file containing 1500 lines of uncommented code. That caused some problems for me, which I might do a ramble on in the future. However, as much it was annoying to figure out what is going on, it did teach me about static hosting. What it can do, what it can't. 

Several, much smaller projects later using static hosting, I was finally ready to start making this website.  

I've learned some cool stuff, so I've tried to keep the website as focused on the goal as possible: Hosting the contents I want to put on it. In fact, much of the "function" of the site is not even direction shown to the user. Of course, they are all very much present if you just open the repo on your local machine. 

As you may already know, the minimal skeleton of a static website has: 
  • index.html -- just an entry point for the site. contains all the bits on the screen like buttons, text, layout, etc. 
  • style.css -- Essentially HTML, but the part about how it looks. It controls the colors, fonts, animations. basically the close for the HTML skeleton.
  • script.js -- Where the behavior lives. Stuff like button clicks, game logic (if you are making a browser game), API call. 
This site is as close to that as possible, except with one small twist. I have a local only editor. 

Its pretty weak, but if I ever need to do something fancy, I will just change the code itself. So having this lowers the cost of uploading more blogs on the user a lot. 

I have it for the panels on the homepage too, everything is changeable within a moment notice. All these edits are just saved very simply into a content.json, which is used as a reference to render the website. Because it's a JSON, I also get more structured content that are easier to load, which fits the design of "data-driven" site better. Maybe I should've done Markdown, but I am more comfortable with JSON. 

Tradeoffs

As far as I can tell, small projects like this is normal to have a "helper" (in our case, a local editor). It fits the main function of the website -- creation and hosting of content. However, it does mean that I also incur the cost of maintaining this editor side. I have already lost some progress because sync issues (I lost the JSON where the content was held). 

On the other side, there is also existential issues with the static hosting set up. The best part is how little I have to pay attention to security. I know, blasphemy, but it is essentially client side only. Unless my GitHub repo gets attacked in such a way where it changes that, it is just a bunch of info you are accessing. As much as you (the reader) and I (the writer) connects spiritually, our machines do not touch.  Of course, the tradeoff is I will NEVER know who has seen this, or how many people have seen this. Nor can I receive easy feedbacks, unless I take you out of my website (perhaps a google form in the future?). 

What's next?

I want to say the UI is ugly, but its not even good enough to be that. It commits the worst crime of all: Generic. So I've got to learn some properly design and make it better. Other than that, I need more posts! There is not nearly enough stuff on here.

posts.title: 100 endgames
posts.summary: it is so nice to have the data already made for you.
posts.body:
Why?

There are different stages to a game, which once you really start to think about it, its a construct (arbitrarily invented stage) of a construct (a game that we invent for entertainment). Nevertheless, constructs, especially ones that divides things, helps with understanding. Endgame is such a construct for weiqi. 

A reddit user by the name of u/M2U2 uploaded 1 9x9 endgame puzzle to reddit every week, for 100 weeks. These are then turned into SGF (a weiqi game file), that are commented and annotated. Essentially, majority of the work. All I wanted to solve is the friction of having to drop the files into a file viewer. 

 The GitHub Repo: 

Repo

Try it yourself:

View page

Some Unique bits

Decided to have 3 modes of operation: Exploration, Responsive, and answer key. 

With exploration, you just browse through the SGFs. This is so we don't randomly lose features by wrapping it in a tool. This is still better than drag and dropping them individually, and behaves close to how a book would as you go through the pages. 

Responsive is the typical puzzle. Where the starting is the "ask", and the user answers. The user plays only one side and receives feedback with whether they match the solution variation or not. 

Answer key I tried to explore a little more, where you play for both sides. However, endgame often have many "0 point moves", and they aren't necessarily mistakes but it does break the variation checks. Also the same result can arrive with different sequence/shape, so this could be very good for simpler shapes, but with full board questions its not as good as it can be. 

There are some standard puzzle behavior too, like shuffle of puzzle order and random orientations. 

Next steps

If, SGF is all I need, could I make my own puzzle site? 
Could I overthrow weiqi 101 as the best puzzle site??
I guess we will find out.

posts.title: Multiplayer clocks (like more than 2!)
posts.summary: A project that seems easy but made me question what is real

posts.title: Notes on Voice
posts.summary: A draft about building a writing voice by collecting pressures instead of chasing style.
posts.body:
Starting point

I used to think voice was a finish you applied at the end. It feels closer to pressure now: what you notice, what you omit, what rhythm you trust, and how much risk you allow into the sentence.

Working rule

Write closer to the real temperature of the thought. Clean it up later, but do not cool it down too early.
